sales.title:
BAKTRIA. Local Issues. AR Didrachm (7.31 gms), ca. 295-285 B.C. NGC AU, Strike: 5/5 Surface: 2/5.
HGC-12, 5; SNG ANS-6. Obverse: Helmeted head of Athena right; Reverse: Owl standing right, head facing; prow and grape bunch to left. A charming local Baktrian issue, showcasing an unusual style quite distinct from the famous Athenian "Owls" that it imitates. Well-centered on its round flan, with sharp detail and a pleasant charcoal tone.
From the Peter Jones M.D. Collection (Greek Coin Art pg. 265; #3214).

Ex: Roma XXVII (3/2023) Lot # 404 (with ticket).
To view all items from the Peter Jones M.D. Collection, click here.

sales.title:
Cyprus, Salamis. Evagoras I. Silver Stater (10.71 g), ca. 411-374 BC. Head of Herakles right, wearing lion's skin headress. Reverse: Ba si le fo se eu (in combined Cypriot and Greek letters), he-goat with long horns and beard seated right on dotted ground line; above, barley corn; to right, letter. BMC 55. Masson & Amandry 36, Ba. NGC grade XF; Strike: 5/5, Surface: 2/5, Brushed. Estimated Value $2,500 - UP
Although his family claimed descent from Teucer, a hero of the Trojan war, and had long ruled in Salamis, Evagoras was forced into exile as a child when the city fell under Phoenician domination. However, in 411, he returned to Salamis with only a band of 50 men and managed to reclaim the throne for himself. As king, he actively cultivated a friendly relationship with Athens by shipping grain and providing a safe haven for the Athenian general Conon in the aftermath of the disastrous Battle of Aigospotamoi (405 BC), which spelled the end of the Athenian Empire and Spartan victory in the Peloponnesian War (431-404 BC). His court also became a second home for many subsequent Athenian emigres. During the Corinthian War (395-387 BC), Evagoras provided most of the ships that Conon used to defeat the Spartan fleet at the Battle of Cnidus (394 BC) and, in turn, received support from Athens and the Egyptian pharaoh Achoris to expand his kingdom at the expense of the Persian Empire. By 391 BC, he controlled almost all of Cyprus as well as parts of the facing Phoenician coast and had raised revolts against Persian domination in Cilicia. Unfortunately, he was largely abandoned by the Athenians once the Corinthian War was brought to an end by the Peace of Antalcidas (387 BC)â€”a settlement that recognized all Greek cities of Asia as subjects of the Persian King. Unsurprisingly, Evagoras I refused to accept the peace treaty and opposed Persian overlordship, but without allies beyond Egypt, which was also threatened, he could not hold out indefinitely. In 382 BC, the Persian satraps Tiribazus and Orontes mounted an invasion of Cyprus with a large army and destroyed Evagoras' fleet at the Battle of Citium the following year. The king escaped back to Salamis, where he used the jealousy between the satraps to gain a favorable peace settlement that left him in power in Salamis at the price of paying tribute to Persia. As a Persian vassal king, Evagoras did not rule long. In 374 BC, he was murdered by a court eunuch over a personal insult. Evagoras was greatly praised for his Hellenism during his lifetime, most notably by the Attic orator Isocrates, who also saw him as the potential leader for a pan-Hellenic crusade against the Persian Empire. However, the present didrachm reveals how bound to tradition Evagoras I also was. While the obverse type of Heracles is very Greek and his name is abbreviated by the Greek letters EY on the reverse, his name is still written out in full using the Cypriot syllabic scriptâ€”a form of writing used on the island since the eleventh century BC.
